content.full_text:
BELL & HILTACHK
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

555 Capitol Mall
Suite 530
Sacramento CA 95814

Charles H. Bell, Jr.
Thomas W. Hiltachk

Telephone: (916) 442-7757
Facsimile: (916) 442-7759

March 20, 1992

Hand Delivered

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Eighth Floor
Sacramento CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This law firm represents "Rancho Mirage Common Sense", an
unincorporated association (referred to informally as a "citizen
educational committee") which is concerned with public issues in
the City of Rancho Mirage. Rancho Mirage Common Sense is
currently exploring the possibility of seeking tax exempt status
under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The
organization takes a vital interest in issues before the City
Council of the City of Rancho Mirage, and communicates with the
general public about issues facing the city, urging the public to
get involved by attending city council and planning commission
meetings and to register to vote. A copy of Vol 1, No. 1 of the
organization's newsletter is attached. While this is the first
issue of the newsletter, Rancho Mirage Common Sense intends to
publish a newsletter regularly, although as yet it has not
determined with what regularity to publish: that may be largely
a function of its resources. As you can see, the newsletter
provides information on a number of issues before the city,
including municipal acquisition of public art, taxes and
spending. Rancho Mirage Common Sense is much like the Diamond
Bar Gadfly Association referenced in Letter to Al Rumpillia, No.
A-91-558.

The organization will not endorse or oppose candidates, in
the City of Rancho Mirage or elsewhere. However, the
organization has in the first newsletter commented on actions and

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 20, 1992
Page 2


omissions of public officials and will in the future comment,
criticize or praise actions of the members of the City Council on
the basis of their positions on issues of interest to Rancho
Mirage Common Sense.

     Rancho Mirage Common Sense requests advice on whether the
organization is a committee and thus must file a Statement of
Organization and periodic reports under the Political Reform Act.
The City of Rancho Mirage has a local campaign ordinance which,
among other things, seems to follow the Political Reform Act
definitions of "committee", "contribution" and "expenditure"
(Rancho Mirage Ord., Ch. No. 2.56.020, Subdivisions E, F, and I,
respectively). The City also has an election, I understand, for
two city council positions, on April 14, 1992. The City Clerk of
the City of Rancho Mirage is of the view that "derogatory
comments" concerning an officeholder or a person who may be a
candidate for elective office in the newsletter's discussion of
public issues would require such filings.

     Rancho Mirage Common Sense does not believe that it is a
committee, or that its activities implicate the Political Reform
Act. As noted above, although the newsletter is critical--indeed
highly critical in some instances--of public officials, it is all
with respect to their official actions and does not "expressly
advocate" the election or defeat of a clearly identified
candidate for office.

     The mere mention of a candidate's name in a publication does
not mean that an organization becomes a committee. Moreover, the
fact that the mention might be considered "derogatory" by one
observer does not amount to "express advocacy" of the election or
defeat of a clearly identified candidate as is required under
California Code of Regs. (Govt. Code § 82025; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
§18225(b)(2).) Moreover, we do not believe comment on issues or
mention of public officials' conduct is more that "grass roots
lobbying", and is clearly not for a political purpose (Govt. Code
§82015, also Rancho Mirage Ord., Ch. 2.56.020 F).

     The Commission's recent advice letter to Mr. Rumpilla is
instructive. The Commission advised Mr. Rumpilla that
expenditures for a video tape which "features a general plan and
identifies several issues of concern to the community" did not
create a filing obligation since the video "does not support or
oppose candidates or ballot measures" (Letter to Al Rumpilla,
supra). Federal courts have upheld the distinction between
"express advocacy" and fair comment (See, FEC v. C.L.I.T.R.I.M.,
616 F. 2d 45 (2d. Cir. 1979); FEC v. National Organization for
Women, 713 F. Supp. 428 (D.D.C. 1989).)

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 20, 1992
Page 3


     We would appreciate your response by telephone if possible,
because the organization has spent over $1,000 and, if required
to register and report, would face a present filing
responsibility.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          [signature]
                                          Charles H. Bell, Jr.

CHB/man
1064.01
Enclosures
